title: The Cash in Your Account That Was Never Actually Yours
linkedin:
Log into internet banking, see a healthy number, and relax a little. Most owner-led businesses treat that figure as the answer. It rarely is.

A meaningful share of what sits in most operating accounts was never the business's to spend. GST collected on invoices. PAYG withheld from wages before it reaches employees. Superannuation guarantee accrued but not yet paid to a fund. All three sit in the same account as trading revenue and look identical on a banking app.

Here's the number worth knowing. For a GST-registered business, roughly one dollar in every eleven collected on a GST-inclusive sale belongs to the ATO, not the business. It accumulates quietly between BAS periods, then leaves in one lump sum when the quarterly payment falls due.

The blind spot is sharpest right now. The June quarter BAS and super have just been paid, the account looks lighter for a few days, then trading cash flows back in through July with no due date in sight until late in the month. The balance climbs, and it is tempting to read that as headroom, when a real share of it is next quarter's GST and super already accruing again.

This is not a discipline problem. It is a structural one, because nothing in a bank account distinguishes held funds from trading cash.

The fix is a running number, calculated monthly: bank balance, minus GST accrued since the last BAS, minus PAYG withheld and unremitted, minus super accrued and unpaid. What is left is what a business can actually commit to spending.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Ever looked at your bank balance after EOFY and felt like you'd turned a corner, only for things to tighten up again a few weeks later for no obvious reason?

Here's what's usually going on. GST collected on sales, tax withheld from wages, and super accrued for staff all sit in the same account as your trading cash. None of it is flagged separately. It just looks like money you have.

Roughly one dollar in every eleven of GST-inclusive sales belongs to the ATO, not the business, and it quietly builds up again from day one of the new financial year.

Do you track your "real" available cash separately from your total balance, or does the bank figure do the talking? Genuinely curious how other owners handle this one.

#CashFlow
google:
A note for East Coast business owners this week: it's worth checking whether your bank balance is telling you the whole story.

A good share of what sits in most operating accounts was never really the business's to spend. GST collected on sales, tax withheld from employee wages, and superannuation guarantee accrued but not yet paid all sit in the same account as everyday trading cash. None of it looks any different on a banking app.

For a GST-registered business, roughly one dollar in every eleven collected on a GST-inclusive sale belongs to the ATO rather than the business. It builds up quietly between BAS periods and leaves the account in one lump sum when the quarterly payment falls due. Superannuation guarantee behaves the same way, accruing every pay run but only leaving the account quarterly.

The businesses that avoid a nasty surprise are the ones who calculate a running "true cash" figure each month: bank balance, minus GST owed, minus tax withheld, minus super accrued. What is left is the number that actually reflects what can be spent.

We work with owner-led businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria on exactly this kind of cash visibility. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
